headline: Venezuela earthquakes: 'The rescue workers didn't try anything. They say it's too dangerous. But we keep going'
subtitle: For the past five days, in La Guaira, the area hit hardest by the two earthquakes, families of victims buried beneath collapsed buildings have worked without pause to pull relatives from the rubble.

France passes watered-down legislation to curb fast fashion
subtitle: The law aimed at 'reducing the environmental impact of the textile industry' passed its final reading in the Sénat on June 29. Its content underwent numerous revisions over the past two years, particularly to accommodate domestic players in France, while excluding online giants Shein and Temu from those concessions.

headline: 'The heatwave shows, beyond any doubt, that without strong mitigation measures, adaptation will be impossible'
subtitle: Efforts to combat climate change require far more than mere technical adjustments, writes Le Monde's Stéphane Foucart in his column.

headline: Six Bad Bunny songs to know before his concerts in France
subtitle: The Puerto Rican superstar is set to perform at the Stade-Vélodrome in Marseille on July 1 and at the Plenitude Arena in Nanterre on July 4 and 5.

headline: In the DRC's Ebola epicenter, a 'cascade of deaths' and fear of contamination
subtitle: Le Monde traveled to Mongbwalu, a town in the eastern Democratic Republic of the Congo, where the Ebola virus outbreak began. As the death toll continues to rise, local health facilities and NGOs have become overwhelmed, hampered by a shortage of resources.
byline: Morgane Le Cam and  Philémon Barbier

subtitle: The Israeli human rights organization B'Tselem documented the deaths of 54 minors killed by the Israeli army in the occupied West Bank in January 2025 and the impunity enjoyed by its soldiers.

headline: 250 years of American independence: Massacre on the prairie
subtitle: 'America 250' (8/13). While US Army troops and those of the Confederacy fought each other, another conflict was taking place on the plains of the American West, against Native Americans. In November 1864, at Sand Creek, in what became Colorado in 1876, the thirst for gold and land drove settlers to barbarism.

headline: At the Four Seasons in Damascus, power shifts but the elite remain
subtitle: A symbol of Bashar al-Assad's grip on Syria, the luxury hotel was the scene of lavish parties and business dealings until the fall of the dictator in December 2024. After briefly serving as the headquarters for the new leaders, it is now a networking hub for entrepreneurs seeking business opportunities in a country where everything must be rebuilt.
